data.content_ref: inline:{"claim":"Systemic interdependence and brittle redistributive economies made palace civilizations vulnerable to cascading failure from climatic-triggered agricultural shortfalls, while Egypt's dispersed irrigation agriculture and private entrepreneurship buffered against monoculture collapse","mechanism":"centralized palace economies with specialized production and long-distance trade created structural fragility; drought-induced subsistence crisis broke patronage obligations; Egypt's mixed economy and private merchant activity (ʿnḫ, pr ḏt) provided alternative coordination mechanisms","scope":{"covers":["Hittite grain dependence on Anatolian plateau","Mycenaean palatial redistribution","Ugarit commercial intermediation","Levantine port specialization","Egyptian private landholding and temple estates"],"excludes":["Neo-Assyrian revival mechanisms","Mycenaean post-palatial continuity sites"]},"forbidden":[{"case":"demonstration that Egyptian central administration collapsed contemporaneously with full territorial fragmentation comparable to Hittite or Mycenaean cases","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"evidence of robust private economic activity continuing uninterrupted in Ugarit or Hattusa during palatial collapse","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"paleoclimatic reconstruction showing 1200-1150 BC as unremarkable climatically in Eastern Mediterranean core zones","eval":"rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes":"Endogenous structuralist account drawing on Polanyi-Dalton substantivism and New Institutional Economics; emphasizes Egyptian 'dual economy' as resilience factor; vulnerable to criticism for functionalism and difficulty specifying triggering threshold"}
